rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is `explicit_self`, so this should remain in the climate-relevant set despite the token-miss gate. The policy text explicitly targets nature/land outcomes: “environmental stewardship and landscape health,” implemented through “community education and engagement,” which supports improved landscape stewardship consistent with the assigned Nature-Based Solutions category.
policy_statement: Cultivate an ethic of environmental stewardship and landscape health through community education and engagement.
mechanism_description: The policy cultivates environmental stewardship through community education and engagement to support healthier landscapes.
primary_causal_pathway: Community engagement/behavior change → improved land stewardship → ecosystem enhancement → ↑ carbon storage/sinks + ↓ land degradation (often ↓ heat/erosion impacts)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: Community knowledge, norms, and participation in land management (e.g., yard/park practices, invasive control, tree/vegetation care, soil protection, reporting/volunteering, compliance with conservation practices).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced disturbance and improved vegetation/soil health increases biomass growth and soil organic carbon retention; healthier landscapes can also enhance evapotranspiration and ground cover, lowering surface temperatures and reducing erosion/runoff.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased/maintained carbon sequestration and avoided emissions from land degradation (and, in some contexts, reduced urban heat and improved resilience of ecosystems to climate stress).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved ecosystem condition that increases or preserves carbon sinks (and co-benefits like cooler microclimates) through better land stewardship behaviors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Education/engagement → stewardship behavior change → improved ecosystem condition → increased carbon sinks (and related biophysical cooling/soil-water benefits).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the policy instrument is “education,” the climate-relevant impact occurs via ecosystems (vegetation/soil) rather than primarily via reduced energy use (Resource Efficiency) or direct emissions controls (Mitigation). Adaptation co-benefits (heat/erosion reduction) are common but not the single dominant effect across all such stewardship-oriented landscape outcomes.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an education program is explicitly aimed at reducing fossil fuel use (e.g., home energy education) it would shift categories; here, the stated target is “landscape health,” which routes through ecosystem enhancement.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses community environmental education/engagement to improve landscape stewardship relies on ecosystem improvement as the central mediator, fitting Nature-Based Solutions as the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the main outcome is carbon sequestration, urban heat reduction, wildfire risk reduction, or watershed benefits depends on policy-specific design and evidence and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “stewardship” is implemented mainly as operational maintenance with negligible ecosystem change (or focuses on litter/beautification without ecological function), the NbS linkage can weaken; conversely, if paired with explicit restoration/urban forestry targets, the NbS classification strengthens.
co_benefits: Biodiversity and habitat improvement; reduced erosion/runoff and improved water quality; urban cooling/heat reduction; recreation/amenity and community cohesion
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a broader planning-strategy (norm-building/engagement) rather than pure information-disclosure; no explicit climate objective stated, so climate relevance is classified as indirect.
